I think it is a very valuable asset once built. And all of the things you name, certifications, levels with prerequisites, distance in live learning, feeding into coaching, alumni, societies, accreditation, university bookstore, on and on and on. I think all of those are the component parts. The thing that either makes it work or doesn't make it work is a sense that it's real, a sense that this is the thing in our business we do. The chiropractors in the room and the people that work with the chiropractic profession, there's a thing in chiropractor called the Parker Foundation. And it doesn't have the power it once had, but for almost a decade, Parker was, if you were a chiropractor, what you did is you went to Parker. That's what you did. And so the conversation was, you go to Parker, of course I'm going to Parker, I'll see you at Parker. I mean, everybody went to Parker and the Parker Foundation was like viewed as something it wasn't. I mean, it was a thing that the Parker family put together to make money. That's what it was. But it had an accepted position in the industry. It has this benign foundation and premier training organization, an event of the year, in a sense the National Convention of the Profession. The Maddow brothers who managed to do it in dentistry to some extent. And so Parker was, I mean, at its peak, 35,000 people in chiropractor, 35,000 people at Parker. And I mean, you went for five days and every vendor exhibited, I mean, they took over Vegas. And they got there, slow and steady and painstaking, but a huge asset, huge money. Because you now have, you know, vendors will give you money when they won't give us money under normal circumstances. Big companies will come play when big companies won't play with us and so forth. So the second thing is creating the position of this is what you do. If you're in the, you know, they don't get it quite done, but it's sort of the position the chamber tries to take on a local basis. And the position the Better Business Bureau tries to take is, oh, you're a small business in Cleveland. What you do is you join the Cleveland Better Business Bureau. That's what you do. It's not like it's an option. It's like that's what you do, you know. And so that's the second thing that you, you know, that you try and get to. And then third is sort of optional but helpful is if the thing delivers something to them that is actually useful in the marketplace in either getting business or getting better business or making a sale, you know. That's the real purpose is to make them better. You're able to brand a, you're able to create a credibility device for them that is actually helpful in their business. Has some teeth can be taken away from them. Those three component parts. Okay. Thank you. You sir. I have a couple. Oh, now you're not allowed to refer to notes, man. We ain't got all day here. Prior to the break, you talked about cold calling, acquiring customers that method. When you do that or when you did that, did you actually say cold call? I said get them cold, but get them cold. There's a difference getting them cold. Did you then put them in your intake process or what happened once you made the contact you sent them in the material got the first meeting? How did it? Well, as I said, you can't. You lose the ability to do much takeaway positioning when you're doing that going directly at them cold because it's so incongruent that you can't flip them on a dime. So when you're in one way shape or form trying to get to a face to face, it's hard to simultaneously be doing takeaway. Now, if you sneak up entirely, so you're going in to interview the CEO for a book on that auspices and now good conversation occurs and gee, maybe we should do something together and so forth. You could take him back out now, drop him through a process. But if you're going to an appointment and now you're going in fundamentally to do a sales presentation, who's kidding who you're going in to do a sales presentation.
